elements.sent-2411-001: Assuming that (quasi-)de Sitter vacua are quantum mechanically described by a finite-dimensional Hilbert space, we develop a detailed framework for making explicit anthropic predictions.
elements.sent-2411-002: A key challenge which we attempt to overcome arises because, almost unavoidably, cosmologies that asymptote to Minkowski space exist.
elements.sent-2411-003: We then apply our framework to predicting the scale of inflation.
elements.sent-2411-004: We find that, even if eternal inflation is allowed, our predictions depend on creation rates of universes from nothing.
elements.sent-2411-005: These, in turn, are highly sensitive to the existence of end-of-the-world branes.
elements.sent-2411-006: The rates for the creation of universes from nothing are the dominant ingredient for `Swampy Landscapes', which may have no metastable de Sitter vacua but only slow-roll solutions.
elements.sent-2411-007: In `Rocky Landscapes', where long-lived de Sitter vacua are abundant, tunneling rates between such vacua represent a further key factor for deriving predictions.. *{.4cm.}
elements.sent-2411-008: For theories exhibiting different vacua, a measure on their set is required to achieve predictivity.
elements.sent-2411-009: If these vacua are continuously populated by eternal inflation, by creation from nothing, or by some other process, this leads to a formidable theoretical challenge known as the measure problem.
elements.sent-2411-010: This was initially studied in the context of eternal inflation \cite{Linde:1993nz and became increasingly more prominent with the advent of the string landscape \cite{Bousso:2000xa, Kachru:2003aw, Susskind:2003kw, Denef:2004ze, Balasubramanian:2005zx (see \cite{Schellekens:2015zua, Hebecker:2020aqr for reviews).}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The reason is the initial expectation that de Sitter (dS) vacua are abundant and eternal inflation \cite{Steinhardt:1982kg, Vilenkin:1983xq, Guth:2007ng is natural.}
elements.sent-2411-012: This perspective has changed more recently with the realization that stringy dS solutions are very difficult to realize and the proposal that they may be entirely absent \cite{Danielsson:2018ztv, Obied:2018sgi, Garg:2018reu, Ooguri:2018wrx, Dvali:2018jhn .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Earlier studies have argued that dS is problematic for exponentially long lifetimes, see e.g. \cite{Ford:1984hs,Mottola:1984ar,Antoniadis:1985pj,Tsamis:1992sx,Goheer:2002vf, Polyakov:2007mm,Dvali:2013eja, Dvali:2014gua, Dvali:2017eba .}
elements.sent-2411-014: On the other hand, long-lived dS is also taken seriously as a quantum system and studied intensely \cite{Banks:2000fe,Witten:2001kn,Strominger:2001pn,Spradlin:2001pw,Chandrasekaran:2022cip,Ivo:2024ill .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In our present work, we will be agnostic concerning the abundance and longevity of dS vacua.
elements.sent-2411-016: In other words, we will allow both for `swampy’ landscapes (using the terminology of \cite{Blanco-Pillado:2019tdf ) in which dS is either extremely rare or non-existent, and `rocky’ landscapes with abundant dS vacua.}
elements.sent-2411-017: One approach to the measure problem, developed for eternally inflating cosmologies, is to count measurements before some late-time cutoff which regularizes the infinities, see e.g.~ \cite{Linde:1993nz, Linde:1993xx, Garriga:2005av, Bousso:2006ev, Susskind:2007pv, DeSimone:2008bq, Bousso:2008hz, Garriga:2008ks, Harlow:2011az .}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, the cutoff dependence makes this somewhat arbitrary, as explained in the reviews \cite{Freivogel:2011eg, Vilenkin:2006xv .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Other approaches appeal to quantum cosmology \cite{Vilenkin:1994ua, nomura2011physical,Bousso:2011up , Hertog:2011ky, nomura2012static,Hartle:2016tpo or count measurements along the worldline of a single observer moving through the multiverse \cite{Dyson:2002pf, garriga2013watchers, nomura2011physical, nomura2012static .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: More recent work on the measure problem includes \cite{Carifio:2017nyb, Chiang:2018dju, Finn:2018krt, Jain:2019gsq, Khoury:2019yoo, Vilenkin:2019mwc, Khoury:2019ajl,Blanco-Pillado:2019tdf, Kartvelishvili:2020thd,Olum:2021pux,Khoury:2021grg,Khoury:2022ish,Khoury:2023ktz,Friedrich:2022tqk,Ivo:2024ill .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In what follows, we will focus on a measure for the multiverse proposed in \cite{Friedrich:2022tqk , which rests on the `cosmological central dogma' (CCD) \cite{Talks,Banks:2000fe,Susskind:2021yvs : The CCD states that de Sitter (dS) space represents a finite quantum mechanical system with Hilbert space dimension $\exp(S_{dS )$, where $S_{dS}$ is the dS entropy.}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: In the following, we assume that the CCD applies to all solutions with a cosmological horizon (quasi-dS solutions), including inflation and quintessence.
elements.sent-2411-023: For simplicity, we will refer to all solutions of this kind simply as `dS vacua'.
elements.sent-2411-024: Conceptually, the proposal of \cite{Friedrich:2022tqk is inspired by the quantum mechanical approach of \cite{Bousso:2011up,nomura2011physical,nomura2012static , formalizing the latter and trying to develop it to a point where explicit predictions can be derived.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The total Hilbert space is taken to be a direct sum, $\mathcal{H =\bigoplus_{i\in vac \mathcal{H _i$, where $i$ labels the vacua of the underlying landscape.}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: This sum contains both finite-dimensional dS as well as infinite-dimensional Minkowski and AdS subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-027: Furthermore, it is assumed that there exists a unique quantum state of the multiverse, $\Psi\in \mathcal{H}$, obtained by solving the Wheeler-DeWitt (WDW) equation with a source term.
elements.sent-2411-028: This source term characterizes the creation of universes from nothing as well as bubble-of-nothing decays.
elements.sent-2411-029: One may think of the proposal \cite{Friedrich:2022tqk as a `local WDW measure', where the term `local' refers to the fact that only a local, horizon-sized patch of each dS vacuum is relevant in the multiverse dynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Predictions can be made by constructing projection operators $P_\alpha,P_\beta$ on subspaces of interest and evaluating ratios \begin{equation}\frac{\bra{\Psi}P_\alpha\ket{\Psi}}{\bra{\Psi}P_\beta\ket{\Psi}}\,.\label{Prediction_Intro}\end{equation} Note that only relative probabilities of the form \eqref{Prediction_Intro can be calculable and physically meaningful.}
elements.sent-2411-031: However, not all such ratios are a priori well-defined since, due to the presence of infinite-dimensional ${\cal H _i$, the wave function $\Psi$ is in general not normalizable.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Clearly, the structure of the underlying Hilbert space and the associated observables depend on UV physics, such that a direct construction of $\Psi$ and the relevant projection operators appears impossible.
elements.sent-2411-033: However, the structure of $\Psi$ can partially be determined semiclassically \cite{Friedrich:2022tqk : The probability distribution of dS vacua in the landscape may be obtained by solving an appropriate rate equation.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The required input parameters are various vacuum creation, decay and transition rates.
elements.sent-2411-035: This method does not allow to ascribe probabilities to Minkowski/AdS vacua since the projection of $\Psi$ on such vacua is in general not normalizable.
elements.sent-2411-036: We note that similar rate equations have appeared before in the context of `local measures', in particular the `causal patch measure' \cite{Bousso:2006ev .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Such measures are defined by counting events along the worldline of an observer \cite{Garriga:2005av, Bousso:2006ev, Bousso:2008hz, Bousso:2010im, garriga2013watchers .}

elements.sent-2411-039: Nevertheless, the local WDW measure differs from this class since it is formulated observer-independently, on the basis of quantum mechanical principles.
elements.sent-2411-040: Observers make their appearance only once projections on appropriate Hilbert subspaces are introduced to derive anthropic predictions.
elements.sent-2411-041: Some of the differences between traditional local measures and the local WDW measure will play a key role in this work.
elements.sent-2411-042: The measure proposal of \cite{nomura2011physical, nomura2012static , while conceptually related to our approach, also depends on an a priori chosen observer.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Additionally, it does not involve the creation of vacua from nothing, which is by contrast of central importance in our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-044: In this work, we explore the phenomenology and fundamental issues of the local WDW measure.
elements.sent-2411-045: It is clear that the finiteness of predictions of the form \eqref{Prediction_Intro is under threat because $\Psi$ is in general not normalizable.}
elements.sent-2411-046: However, when considering anthropic predictions, the projection operators in \eqref{Prediction_Intro need to involve a projection on observers, which may restore finiteness.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Since, arguably, the leading paradigm for creating anthropic observers is a period of inflation followed by reheating and structure formation, we focus on such corners of the landscape.
elements.sent-2411-048: If observers live in asymptotic dS vacua, i.e.~if a (small) cosmological constant is present, we can approximate the observer projection as follows: First, we project on the pure dS-part of the Hilbert space which is finite dimensional and hence leads to well-defined predictions of the form \eqref{Prediction_Intro .}
elements.sent-2411-049: For observers formed after a reheating period, we then project further on the subspace of states describing inflation.
elements.sent-2411-050: We conclude that the probability $p({\rm obs ,i)$ of an observer living in vacuum $i$ is, to leading order, given by \begin{equation}p({\rm obs},i)\propto p_{\rm inf (i)}\,,\label{Intro_p_o_i}\end{equation} the probability of finding the universe in an inflationary state leading to reheating in vacuum~$i$.}

elements.sent-2411-085: This article is organized as follows: In Sect.~\ref{sect_Local_WDW_review , we briefly review the local WDW measure and explain how elementary anthropic predictions can be made.}
elements.sent-2411-086: In Sect.~\ref{sect_projecting_on_observers , we discuss the problem of infinitely many observers living in terminal vacua and provide possible resolutions.}
elements.sent-2411-087: In Sect.~\ref{sect_strat , we discuss the fundamental ingredients needed to predict the most likely scale of inflation to be measured by anthropic observers.}
elements.sent-2411-088: In Sect.~\ref{sect_String_rates , we explicitly calculate various vacuum decay rates for KKLT and LVS type vacua.}
elements.sent-2411-089: In Sect.~\ref{sect_explicit_predictions , we combine the results of the previous sections to try and make an explicit prediction for the scale of inflation relevant for anthropic observers in a string multiverse.}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Anthropic predictions from the local WDW measure for observers in dS universes

elements.section-2411-003: Projecting on observers in the presence of terminal vacua
elements.sent-2411-133: In the previous section, we have seen how to make predictions in case observers can only live in asymptotically dS vacua.
elements.sent-2411-134: In this section, we will discuss the general situation, allowing for observers in terminal vacua.
elements.sent-2411-135: This will lead to difficulties in particular in the case of observers in Minkowski space, to which we refer as the `Minkowski-space infinity problem'.
elements.sent-2411-136: Subsect.~\ref{sect_summary_on_counting provides an executive summary of the main results of this discussion.}
elements.subsection-2411-003: Summary of approaches to the Minkowski-space infinity-problem
elements.sent-2411-137: If observers can exist in AdS and Minkowski vacua, the finiteness argument based on the finite dimensionality of dS Hilbert spaces fails.
elements.sent-2411-138: In the case of AdS, this may be argued to be irrelevant since the unavoidable big crunch allows only finite portions of a reheating surface in AdS to be causally connected.
elements.sent-2411-139: For a reheating surface in Minkowski space, no such argument can be made.
elements.sent-2411-140: Moreover, even if no Minkowski-space reheating surfaces with observers exist, the Minkowski-space infinity problem still persists because of bubble collisions: In such collisions, infinite parts of dS-space reheating surfaces can make it into a Minkowski region \cite{Kleban:2011pg .}
elements.sent-2411-141: We explain this in more detail in Sects.~\ref{fbfh -\ref{noet and analyze possible resolutions.}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Let us here give a brief and systematic summary, following the diagram in Fig.~\ref{fig_tree_diagram :.}
elements.sent-2411-143: No Minkowski space: The issue disappears if Minkowski vacua are in the Swampland.
elements.sent-2411-144: Finite number of observers in Minkowski space: Two lines of argument may avoid observer-infinities in Minkowski space: \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-145: Effective CCD: The CCD declares that only a finite portion of any spacelike surface in dS is physical.
elements.sent-2411-146: By analogy, we propose that only a finite portion of any reheating surface, including in Minkowski space, contains independent observers.
elements.sent-2411-147: No reward for infinitely many observers: Infinite observer counts may be irrelevant for probabilistic predictions.
elements.sent-2411-148: This may either hold as a postulate \cite{Hertog:2013mra,Hartle:2016tpo or because observers contain finitely many bits: They can then be grouped in a finite set of equivalence classes, which we call `dressed finite observers' or `DFOs'.. \end{itemize.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Taking infinities seriously: If infinities of observers in Minkowski space do indeed affect predictions, this may happen in two distinct ways (or through a combination thereof): \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Predicting reheating to Minkowski: If reheating to Minkowski is possible, then anthropic arguments predict Minkowski space in our asymptotic future, suggesting quintessence-like dark energy in our Universe.

elements.subsection-2411-004: Finiteness based on a finite observer Hilbert space

elements.section-2411-004: Explicit predictions in the multiverse -- basic ingredients

elements.sent-2411-257: This equation can be solved in the form of the series (see Appendix \ref{appendix_solving_rate_equation for more details): \begin{equation}p_i = \sum_{N=0}^\infty p_{i,N}\,, \quad p_{i,0}=\frac{J_i}{\Gamma_i}\,,\quad p_{i,N}=\frac{1}{\Gamma_i} \sum_{j_1,...,j_N}J_{j_1}\frac{\Gamma_{j_1\to j_2}}{\Gamma_{j_1}}\frac{\Gamma_{j_2\to j_3}}{\Gamma_{j_2}}\hdots \frac{\Gamma_{j_N\to i}}{\Gamma_{j_N}}\,.\label{p_i_solution}\end{equation} We note that the structure of \eqref{p_i_solution is straightforward to interpret.}}

elements.sent-2411-260: It becomes apparent that we need to investigate both the creation rates $J_i$ as well as the tunneling rates $\Gamma_{i\to j}$ in more detail.
elements.sent-2411-261: The traditional proposals for the creation of universes from nothing are the Hartle-Hawking no boundary \cite{hartle1983wave and the Linde/Vilenkin tunneling \cite{Linde:1983mx,Vilenkin:1984wp proposals.}}
elements.sent-2411-262: Both study the quantum mechanical nucleation of compact spherical universes which eventually go on shell and expand.
elements.sent-2411-263: In both cases, the basic ingredient is an instanton geometry which describes euclidean dS, i.e.~a 4-sphere.
elements.sent-2411-264: Its action $\mathcal{S}$ is given by minus the dS entropy, $\mathcal S=-S_{ds}$, with \begin{equation}S_{dS}=24\pi^2M_P^4/V_{dS}=8\pi^2M_P^2\ell_{dS}^2\,.\label{S_Entropy_form}\end{equation} Here $V_{dS}$ is the energy density and $\ell_{dS}$ the dS radius.
elements.sent-2411-265: The proposed rates are \begin{equation}J\propto  \exp(S_{dS}) \text{Hartle-Hawking \cite{Hawking:1998bn}} \exp(-S_{dS}) \text{Linde/Vilenkin \cite{Linde:1983mx, Vilenkin:1984wp}} \, .\label{source_strength}\end{equation} Clearly Hartle-Hawking favor small and Linde/Vilenkin favor large vacuum energies.
elements.sent-2411-266: A different proposal for creating universes from nothing was introduced in \cite{Hawking:1998bn,Turok:1998he and further developed in \cite{Garriga:1998ri,Bousso:1998pk,Blanco-Pillado:2011fcm .}}
elements.sent-2411-267: Its key ingredient are end-of-the-world (ETW) branes, representing boundaries of 4d spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-268: The existence of such boundaries allows for the nucleation of universes with the geometry of a ball, bounded by a spherical ETW brane.
elements.sent-2411-269: The prime example of an ETW brane arises in 5d to 4d compactifications, where the $S^1$ compact space can shrink to zero size at a codimension-one locus over the 4d base.
elements.sent-2411-270: This gives rise to Witten's famous `bubble of nothing' \cite{Witten:1981gj .}

elements.sent-2411-564: Solving the rate equation \eqref{rate_equation amounts to inverting the matrix $M=X+Y$ with \begin{equation}X_{ij}=\delta_{ij}\Gamma_i\,,\quad Y_{ij}=-\Gamma_{j\to i}\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-565: Formally, the inverse is given by \begin{equation}(X+Y)^{-1}=X^{-1}-X^{-1}YX^{-1}+X^{-1}YX^{-1}YX^{-1}-...\,.\label{lemma_inverse_of_matrix_sum}\end{equation} To see that this series converges and $M$ is hence indeed invertible (an observation also made in \cite{Garriga:2005av ), we write \begin{equation}(YX^{-1})_{ij}=-\sum_k \Gamma_{k\to i}\frac{\delta_{kj}}{\Gamma_j}=-\frac{\Gamma_{j\to i}}{\Gamma_j}\,,\label{calculation_B_Ainverse}\end{equation} and observe that \begin{equation}\forall i,j: \qquad \sum_i|(YX^{-1})_{ij}|<1\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-566: This crucial inequality is a consequence of the sum going only over (approximate) dS solutions, excluding terminal vacua.
elements.sent-2411-567: Given that the standard matrix norm $\Vert A\Vert\equiv \sup_x (\Vert Ax\Vert/\Vert x\Vert )$ satisfies $\Vert A\Vert = \max_j\sum_i|A_{ij |$, we may now conclude that $\Vert YX^{-1}\Vert<1$.}
elements.sent-2411-568: Hence the series \eqref{lemma_inverse_of_matrix_sum converges and \eqref{p_i_solution solves our rate equation.}}
elements.section-2411-009: The decay rate in certain limits
elements.sent-2411-569: For later use, we collect some facts about the tunneling rate \eqref{decay_rate and consider certain limits.}
elements.sent-2411-570: First, we note that $V_f$ and $V_t$ can be expressed using the dimensionless variables \eqref{dimensionless_variables as \begin{equation}V_f=\frac{3T^2(y+1)}{8M_P^2x}\,,\qquad V_t=\frac{3T^2(y-1)}{8M_P^2x}\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-571: The entropy of the false vacuum may be written as \begin{equation}S_f=\frac{64\pi^2 M_P^6x}{T^2(y+1)}\,.\label{appendix_entropy_dimless_variables}\end{equation} Using \begin{equation}\frac{27 T^6}{(V_f-V_t)^3}=64M_P^6x^3\end{equation} and \eqref{appendix_entropy_dimless_variables , the decay exponent \eqref{decay_rate can be given in the form \begin{equation}B=\frac{64M_P^6x^3}{2T^2}r(x,y)=S_f\frac{x^2 (y+1)}{2}r(x,y)=S_f\frac{1+xy-\sqrt{1+2xy+x^2}}{(y-1)\sqrt{1+2xy+x^2}}\,.\label{appendix_general_rate}\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-572: The tunneling process to Minkowski space is of interest for example in the decay to decompactification.
elements.sent-2411-573: For $x\gg y$ and $x\gg 1$, we may characterize $B$ by expanding the function of $x$ and $y$ appearing in \eqref{appendix_general_rate in $1/x$: \begin{equation}B=S_f\left(1-\frac{y+1}{x}+\frac{3y(y+1)}{2x^2}+\order{\frac{1}{x^3}}\right)\,.\label{large_x_xy}\end{equation} Using \eqref{appendix_entropy_dimless_variables and \eqref{dimensionless_variables , one finds \begin{equation}B=S_f-\frac{64\pi^2M_P^6}{T^2}+\frac{128\pi^2M_P^8(V_t+V_f)}{T^4}+\order{1}\,.\label{appendix_large_x_NLO}\end{equation} We see that the dependence of the true vacuum energy density only enters at third order.}}}
elements.section-2411-010: Tunneling rates for KKLT and LVS type vacua
elements.sent-2411-574: We recall that, if two vacua are connected by a barrier in a scalar-field potential $V(\phi)$, then the tension of the corresponding domain wall reads \begin{equation}T=\int_{\phi_f}^{\phi_t} d\phi\, \sqrt{2V(\phi)}\,. \label{Tension_def}\end{equation} Here $\phi_f$ and $\phi_t$ are the field values characterizing the false and true vacuum.
elements.sent-2411-575: Parametrically, the tension obeys \begin{equation}T\sim \Delta \phi \sqrt{V_{barrier}}\,,\label{Tension_def_approx}\end{equation} where $\Delta \phi$ is the width and $V_{barrier}$ the height of the potential barrier.
elements.sent-2411-576: This tunneling transition generically exists due to the typical runaway behavior of the potential at field-space infinity.
elements.sent-2411-577: Equations \eqref{Tension_def , \eqref{Tension_def_approx still hold as an approximation, with $\phi_t$ being replaced by the field value at which the runaway potential has fallen to the value of the false vacuum.}}
elements.sent-2411-578: The width and the height of the barrier and hence the tension $T$ have been estimated in \cite{Kachru:2003aw,Westphal:2007xd .}
elements.sent-2411-579: For KKLT, the barrier height is parametrically given by $\sim M_P^4 g_s|W_0|^2/\vol^2$, where $\vol$ is measured in 10d Planck units .
elements.sent-2411-581: Using the definition \eqref{dimensionless_variables and assuming that $V_f-V_t=V_f\sim M_P^4 g_s|W_0|^2/\vol^2$, we find \begin{equation}x_{dec, KKLT}\sim \frac{M_P^6g_s|W_0|^2 \vol^2}{\vol^{10/3}M_P^6g_s|W_0|^2}\sim \frac{1}{\vol^{4/3}} < 1\label{x_dec_KKLT}\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-582: According to \eqref{appendix_Minkowski_tunneling , the tunneling exponent is given by \begin{equation}B=\frac{S_f}{(1+1/x_{dec, KKLT})^2}\,, \label{B_dec_KKLT}\end{equation} and is thus a few orders of magnitude smaller than the entropy.}
elements.sent-2411-583: Here we also recall that ${\cal V}\sim \ln(1/|W_0|)$ and hence, even if $W_0$ is tuned very small, can never become extremely large.

elements.sent-2411-589: Generically, the parameter $x$ and the decay exponent $B$ are then given by \begin{equation}x_{dec, LVS}\sim {\cal O}(1)\,,\qquad B=\frac{S_f}{(1+1/x_{dec, LVS})^2}\,.\label{B_dec_LVS}\end{equation} We conclude that the decay exponent is of the same magnitude as the entropy.

elements.sent-2411-601: Hence, the domain wall tension satisfies the inequality \begin{equation}T_{D5, D.W.}=\frac{M_P^32\pi g_s^{1/2}}{(4\pi)^{3/2}\vol}\int_\Sigma \sqrt{g_\Sigma}\geq\frac{M_P^32\pi g_s^{1/2}}{(4\pi)^{3/2}\vol}\left|\Delta W\right|\,.\label{tension_bound_D5}\end{equation} If, by analogy, the domain wall is built using an NS5 brane, the $H_3$ flux jumps and the superpotential changes by \begin{equation}\Delta W = \sum_i S\,(r^i_BG_i+s_i^Az^i)\,,\end{equation} with $S$ the axio-dilaton.
